title: Thermodynamic efficiency of self-organisation in nonequilibrium steady states
abstract: Active matter generates order or patterns through nonequilibrium dynamics. An open research challenge is to determine how efficiently a nonequilibrium self-organising system can convert consumed energy into macroscopic order. We study an information-theoretic quantity that directly addresses this challenge by estimating the entropy reduction induced by a small control-parameter perturbation, relative to the generalised work required for the perturbation. This quantity has previously been considered mainly in an equilibrium or near-equilibrium context, and here we extend this framework and apply it to two nonequilibrium self-organising systems: persistent and active Ising models. We observe that the thermodynamic efficiency of nonequilibrium systems maximises at phase transitions, as in equilibrium systems. Furthermore, we compare thermodynamic efficiency and inferential efficiency across control parameters. While these two quantities are equal in equilibrium as a consequence of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, we report that they diverge out of equilibrium, and the gap reflects how far the system is from equilibrium.
summary: This paper investigates the efficiency of energy conversion into macroscopic order in nonequilibrium steady states. It demonstrates that while thermodynamic efficiency peaks at phase transitions, it diverges from inferential efficiency due to the violation of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem out of equilibrium.
main_problem: Determining how efficiently nonequilibrium self-organizing systems convert consumed energy into macroscopic order and investigating the divergence between thermodynamic and inferential efficiency out of equilibrium.
main_result: Thermodynamic efficiency maximizes at phase transitions in both equilibrium and nonequilibrium steady states, while the gap between thermodynamic and inferential efficiency serves as a measure of the system's distance from equilibrium.
method: The study uses Monte Carlo simulations and a refined mean-field approximation (treating density and magnetization as independent Gaussian variables) to analyze two models.
model_or_system: The study focuses on two nonequilibrium models: the Persistent Ising Model, which uses a non-conservative drive to break detailed balance, and the Active Ising Model, which features self-propelled particles on a 2D lattice.
key_observables: Thermodynamic efficiency (eta), inferential efficiency (eta_triangular_triangle), Shannon entropy, infinitesimal work, and magnetization.
important_parameters: Coupling strength (J), external drive (E0), self-propulsion (epsilon), external magnetic field (h), and lattice size.
main_assumptions: The refined mean-field approach assumes density and magnetization are independent Gaussian random variables, which fails to capture the homogeneous bistable phase and becomes inaccurate in strong-coupling regimes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Persistent Ising model dynamics; Figure 7 shows phase plots and profiles for the Active Ising model; Figure 11 demonstrates efficiency peaks at phase transitions; Figure 12 shows finite-size effects on efficiencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of energy-to-order conversion, defines the information-theoretic framework, applies it to the Persistent and Active Ising models, analyzes phase transitions and efficiency divergence, and concludes with a discussion on the Principle of Super-efficiency.
why_interesting: The study provides a fundamental link between thermodynamics and information theory in nonequilibrium systems, which is highly relevant to understanding dissipation and control in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of persistent Ising model. (a) We consider the square-lattice Ising model with nearest-neighbour interactions, modified by a non-conservative drive E0 as described in [30]. The transition probability of a single spin is determined by the effective change in the energy ∆˜E = ∆E + E0, where ∆E is the energy difference before and after the spin-flip. (b) When E0 > 0, there is a cost associated with the spin-flip. Transitions in all directions are inhibited. (c) When E0 < 0, the spins are self-excited. Transitions in all directions are promoted.

FIG. 2: Schematic of active Ising model. The model consists of N spin-carrying and self-propelled particles on a lattice of size Lx × Ly, where each site contains an arbitrary number of particles (left panel). The particles undergo two different dynamics (right panel): spin-flipping and hopping. Local density ρi and local magnetisation mi are measured for each site.

FIG. 3: Phase transition in the persistent Ising model. (a) Plot of control parameter J and its conjugate order parameter ⟨P σiσj/N⟩(average interaction) shows that the system undergoes second-order phase transitions for different values of drive E0. When E0 = 0, the system is at equilibrium, while E0 ̸= 0 represents a nonequilibrium steady state (NESS). The value of J where the phase transition occurs depends on E0. (b) The conjugate order parameter is shown as a function of control parameter J and drive E0. The curve separating two phases of the system represents the critical regime. Lattice size: 50 × 50.

FIG. 4: Thermodynamic efficiency. Thermodynamic efficiencies (η(J)) with respect to control parameter J, at different external drives E0. In all cases, η maximises at the critical control parameter, with negligible discrepancies due to numerical simulation. Lattice size: 50 × 50.

FIG. 5: Thermodynamic efficiency (η) compared with inferential efficiency (η▷◁). (a) At equilibrium, η(J) matches η▷◁(J) for all values of J. (b) In NESS, η(J) ̸= η▷◁(J). When E0 < 0 (red and peach curves), the inferential efficiency (dashed lines) is higher than the thermodynamic efficiency (solid lines). The opposite holds for E0 > 0 (light and dark blue curves). In both equilibrium and NESS, η(J) and η▷◁(J) are optimised at the same control parameter value. Lattice size: 50 × 50.
